title: Scholasticism

infobox.value: is a [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") European philosophical movement or methodology that was the predominant education in Europe from about 1100 to 1700.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is known for employing logically precise analyses toward the goal of reconciling [classical philosophy](/wiki/Classical_philosophy "Classical philosophy") (particularly [Aristotelian logic](/wiki/Aristotelian_logic "Aristotelian logic")) and [Catholic Christianity](/wiki/Catholic_Christianity "Catholic Christianity").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Medieval school of philosophy

**Scholasticism** is a [medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") European philosophical movement or methodology that was the predominant education in Europe from about 1100 to 1700.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is known for employing logically precise analyses toward the goal of reconciling [classical philosophy](/wiki/Classical_philosophy "Classical philosophy") (particularly [Aristotelian logic](/wiki/Aristotelian_logic "Aristotelian logic")) and [Catholic Christianity](/wiki/Catholic_Christianity "Catholic Christianity").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The Scholastics, also known as **Schoolmen**,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) used [dialectical reasoning](/wiki/Dialectical_reasoning "Dialectical reasoning") predicated upon [Aristotelianism](/wiki/Aristotelianism "Aristotelianism") and the [Ten Categories](/wiki/Categories_(Aristotle) "Categories (Aristotle)"). Scholasticism emerged within the [monastic schools](/wiki/Monastic_school "Monastic school") that translated medieval [Judeo-Islamic philosophies](/wiki/Judeo-Islamic_philosophies_(800%E2%80%931400) "Judeo-Islamic philosophies (800–1400)"), and "rediscovered" the [collected works of Aristotle](/wiki/Corpus_Aristotelicum "Corpus Aristotelicum"). Endeavoring to harmonize Aristotle's [metaphysics](/wiki/Metaphysics_(Aristotle) "Metaphysics (Aristotle)") and [Latin Catholic](/wiki/Latin_Catholic "Latin Catholic") theology, these monastic schools became the basis of the earliest European [medieval universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university"), and thus became the bedrock for the development of [modern science](/wiki/History_of_science "History of science") and [philosophy](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy") in the Western world. The rise of scholasticism was closely associated with these schools that flourished in [Italy](/wiki/Italy_in_the_Middle_Ages "Italy in the Middle Ages"), [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Scholasticism is a method of learning more than a philosophy or a theology, since it places a strong emphasis on dialectical reasoning to extend knowledge by [inference](/wiki/Inference "Inference") and to resolve [contradictions](/wiki/Contradictions "Contradictions"). Scholastic thought is also known for rigorous conceptual analysis and the careful drawing of distinctions. In the classroom and in writing, it often takes the form of explicit [disputation](/wiki/Disputation "Disputation"): a topic drawn from the tradition is broached in the form of a proposition on a question to be debated, oppositional responses are given, a counterproposal is argued and oppositional arguments rebutted. Because of its emphasis on rigorous dialectical method, scholasticism was eventually applied to many other fields of study.[[6]](#cite_note-cambridgedict-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Scholasticism was initially a program conducted by medieval Christian thinkers attempting to harmonize the various authorities of their own tradition, and to reconcile Christian theology with classical and late antique philosophy, especially that of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") but also of [Neoplatonism](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Prominent scholastic figures include [Anselm of Canterbury](/wiki/Anselm_of_Canterbury "Anselm of Canterbury") ("the father of scholasticism"[[9]](#cite_note-9)), [Peter Abelard](/wiki/Peter_Abelard "Peter Abelard"), [Alexander of Hales](/wiki/Alexander_of_Hales "Alexander of Hales"), [Albertus Magnus](/wiki/Albertus_Magnus "Albertus Magnus"), [Duns Scotus](/wiki/Duns_Scotus "Duns Scotus"), [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham"), [Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure"), and [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas"). Aquinas's masterwork, *[Summa Theologica](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")* (1265–1274), is widely considered to be the pinnacle of scholastic, medieval, and Christian philosophy.[[10]](#cite_note-GilsonP-10) Important work in the scholastic tradition has been carried on well past Aquinas's time, such as with English scholastics [Robert Grosseteste](/wiki/Robert_Grosseteste "Robert Grosseteste") and his student [Roger Bacon](/wiki/Roger_Bacon "Roger Bacon"), by [Francisco Suárez](/wiki/Francisco_Su%C3%A1rez "Francisco Suárez") and [Luis de Molina](/wiki/Luis_de_Molina "Luis de Molina"), and also among Lutheran and Reformed thinkers.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The terms "scholastic" and "scholasticism" derive from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word **scholasticus**, the Latinized form of the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") σχολαστικός (**scholastikos**), an adjective derived from σχολή (**scholē**), "[school](/wiki/School "School")".[[11]](#cite_note-11) *Scholasticus* means "of or pertaining to schools". The "scholastics", therefore, were roughly "schoolmen".

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The foundations of Christian scholasticism were laid by [Boethius](/wiki/Boethius "Boethius") through his logical and theological essays.[[6]](#cite_note-cambridgedict-6) Later forerunners (and then companions) to scholasticism were Islamic [Ilm al-Kalām](/wiki/Kal%C4%81m "Kalām"), meaning "science of discourse",[[12]](#cite_note-Wolfson-12) and [Jewish philosophy](/wiki/Jewish_philosophy#Jewish_scholarship_after_destruction_of_Second_Temple "Jewish philosophy"), especially [Jewish Kalam](/wiki/Jewish_Kalam "Jewish Kalam").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Early scholasticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early scholasticism")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Anselm_of_Canterbury%2C_seal.svg/250px-Anselm_of_Canterbury%2C_seal.svg.png)

[Anselm of Canterbury](/wiki/Anselm_of_Canterbury "Anselm of Canterbury")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Abelard.jpg/250px-Abelard.jpg)

[Peter Abelard](/wiki/Peter_Abelard "Peter Abelard")

The first significant renewal of learning in the West came with the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance") of the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), advised by [Peter of Pisa](/wiki/Peter_of_Pisa "Peter of Pisa") and [Alcuin of York](/wiki/Alcuin_of_York "Alcuin of York"), attracted the scholars of England and Ireland, where some Greek works continued to survive in the original. By a 787 decree, he established schools at every abbey in his empire. These schools, from which the name *scholasticism* derived,[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Scholasticism#Dubious "Talk:Scholasticism")*] became centers of medieval learning.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

During this period, knowledge of Ancient Greek had vanished in the West except in Ireland, where its teaching and use was fairly common in its [monastic schools](/wiki/Monastic_school "Monastic school").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Irish scholars had a considerable presence in the [Frankish court](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), where they were renowned for their learning.[[16]](#cite_note-StanfordEriugena-16) Among them was [Johannes Scotus Eriugena](/wiki/Johannes_Scotus_Eriugena "Johannes Scotus Eriugena") (815–877), one of the founders of scholasticism.[[17]](#cite_note-Gothic-17) Eriugena was the most significant Irish intellectual of the early monastic period and an outstanding philosopher in terms of originality.[[16]](#cite_note-StanfordEriugena-16) He had considerable familiarity with the Greek language and translated many works into Latin, affording access to the [Cappadocian Fathers](/wiki/Cappadocian_Fathers "Cappadocian Fathers") and the [Greek theological tradition](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Christian_theology "Eastern Orthodox Christian theology").[[16]](#cite_note-StanfordEriugena-16) Three other primary founders of scholasticism were the 11th-century archbishops [Lanfranc](/wiki/Lanfranc_of_Canterbury "Lanfranc of Canterbury") and [Anselm](/wiki/Anselm_of_Canterbury "Anselm of Canterbury") of [Canterbury](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Canterbury "Archdiocese of Canterbury") in [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and [Peter Abelard](/wiki/Peter_Abelard "Peter Abelard") in [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France").[[17]](#cite_note-Gothic-17)

This period saw the beginning of the "[rediscovery](/wiki/Transmission_of_Greek_philosophical_ideas_in_the_Middle_Ages "Transmission of Greek philosophical ideas in the Middle Ages")" of many Greek works which had been lost to the Latin West. As early as the latter half of the 10th century, the [Toledo School of Translators](/wiki/Toledo_School_of_Translators "Toledo School of Translators") in [Muslim Spain](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") had begun translating Arabic texts into Latin.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg197860–61-18) After a successful burst of [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista") in the 12th century, Spain opened even further for Christian scholars and, as these Europeans encountered [Judeo-Islamic philosophies](/wiki/Judeo-Islamic_philosophies_(800%E2%80%931400) "Judeo-Islamic philosophies (800–1400)"), they opened a wealth of Arab and Judaic knowledge of mathematics and astronomy.[[19]](#cite_note-institutions-19) The [Latin translations of the 12th century](/wiki/Latin_translations_of_the_12th_century "Latin translations of the 12th century") also included figures like [Constantine the African](/wiki/Constantine_the_African "Constantine the African") in Italy and [James of Venice](/wiki/James_of_Venice "James of Venice") in Constantinople. Scholars such as [Adelard of Bath](/wiki/Adelard_of_Bath "Adelard of Bath") traveled to Spain and Sicily, translating works on astronomy and mathematics, including the first complete translation of [Euclid](/wiki/Euclid "Euclid")'s *[Elements](/wiki/Euclid%27s_Elements "Euclid's Elements")* into Latin.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClagett1982356-20)

At the same time, the [School of Chartres](/wiki/School_of_Chartres "School of Chartres") produced [Bernard of Chartres](/wiki/Bernard_of_Chartres "Bernard of Chartres")'s commentaries on [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato")'s *[Timaeus](/wiki/Timaeus_(dialogue) "Timaeus (dialogue)")* and a range of works by [William of Conches](/wiki/William_of_Conches "William of Conches") that attempted to reconcile the use of classical pagan and philosophical sources in a medieval Christian concept using the kludge of *integumentum*, treating the obviously [heretical](/wiki/Heresy_in_the_Catholic_Church "Heresy in the Catholic Church") surface meanings as coverings disguising a deeper (and more orthodox) truth.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Abelard himself was condemned by [Bernard of Clairvaux](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux") at the [1141 Council of Sens](/wiki/1141_Council_of_Sens "1141 Council of Sens") and William avoided a similar fate through systematic self-bowdlerization of his early work, but his commentaries and encyclopedic *[De Philosophia Mundi](/wiki/De_Philosophia_Mundi "De Philosophia Mundi")* and *[Dragmaticon](/wiki/Dragmaticon "Dragmaticon")* were miscredited to earlier scholars like [Bede](/wiki/Venerable_Bede "Venerable Bede") and widely disseminated. [Anselm of Laon](/wiki/Anselm_of_Laon "Anselm of Laon") systematized the production of the [gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(annotation) "Gloss (annotation)") on Scripture, followed by the rise to prominence of [dialectic](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") (the middle subject of the medieval [trivium](/wiki/Trivium_(education) "Trivium (education)")) in the work of [Abelard](/wiki/Abelard "Abelard"). [Peter Lombard](/wiki/Peter_Lombard "Peter Lombard") produced a collection of *[Sentences](/wiki/Sentences "Sentences"),* or opinions of the Church Fathers and other authorities.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

More recently, [Leinsle](/w/index.php?title=Ulrich_Leinsle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ulrich Leinsle (page does not exist)"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Novikoff](/w/index.php?title=Alex_Novikoff&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alex Novikoff (page does not exist)"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) and others have argued against the idea that scholasticism primarily derived from philosophical contact, emphasizing its continuity with earlier [Patristic Christianity](/wiki/Patristic_Christianity "Patristic Christianity"). This remains, however, a minority viewpoint.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### High scholasticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: High scholasticism")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Vicente_salvador_gomez-san_alberto.jpg/120px-Vicente_salvador_gomez-san_alberto.jpg)

[Albertus Magnus](/wiki/Albertus_Magnus "Albertus Magnus")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Polittico_del_1476%2C_s._tommaso_d%27aquino.jpg/120px-Polittico_del_1476%2C_s._tommaso_d%27aquino.jpg)

[Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/JohnDunsScotus_-_full.jpg/120px-JohnDunsScotus_-_full.jpg)

[Duns Scotus](/wiki/Duns_Scotus "Duns Scotus")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/William_of_Ockham.png/120px-William_of_Ockham.png)

[William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham")

The 13th and early 14th centuries are generally seen as the high period of scholasticism. The early 13th century witnessed the culmination of the [recovery of Greek philosophy](/wiki/Recovery_of_Aristotle "Recovery of Aristotle"). Schools of translation grew up in Italy and Sicily, and eventually in the rest of Europe. Powerful Norman kings gathered men of knowledge from Italy and other areas into their courts as a sign of their prestige.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindberg197870–72-25) [William of Moerbeke](/wiki/William_of_Moerbeke "William of Moerbeke")'s translations and editions of Greek philosophical texts in the middle half of the thirteenth century helped form a clearer picture of Greek philosophy, particularly of Aristotle, than was given by the Arabic versions on which they had previously relied. [Edward Grant](/wiki/Edward_Grant "Edward Grant") writes "Not only was the structure of the Arabic language radically different from that of Latin, but some Arabic versions had been derived from earlier Syriac translations and were thus twice removed from the original Greek text. Word-for-word translations of such Arabic texts could produce tortured readings. By contrast, the structural closeness of Latin to Greek, permitted literal, but intelligible, word-for-word translations."[[19]](#cite_note-institutions-19)

[Universities](/wiki/University "University") developed in the large cities of Europe during this period, and rival clerical orders within the church began to battle for political and intellectual control over these centers of educational life. The two main orders founded in this period were the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") and the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"). The Franciscans were founded by [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") in 1209. Their leader in the middle of the century was [Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure"), a traditionalist who defended the theology of [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") and the philosophy of [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato"), incorporating only a little of Aristotle in with the more neoplatonist elements. Following Anselm, Bonaventure supposed that reason can only discover truth when philosophy is illuminated by religious faith.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Other important Franciscan scholastics were [Duns Scotus](/wiki/Duns_Scotus "Duns Scotus"), [Peter Auriol](/wiki/Peter_Auriol "Peter Auriol") and [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

By contrast, the Dominican order, a teaching order founded by [St Dominic](/wiki/St_Dominic "St Dominic") in 1215, to propagate and defend Christian doctrine, placed more emphasis on the use of reason and made extensive use of the [new Aristotelian sources](/wiki/Recovery_of_Aristotle "Recovery of Aristotle") derived from the East and Moorish Spain. The great representatives of Dominican thinking in this period were [Albertus Magnus](/wiki/Albertus_Magnus "Albertus Magnus") and (especially) [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas"), whose artful synthesis of Greek rationalism and Christian doctrine eventually came to define Catholic philosophy.

Aquinas's masterwork, *[Summa Theologica](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")* (1265–1274), is considered to be the pinnacle of scholastic, medieval, and Christian philosophy.[[10]](#cite_note-GilsonP-10) It began while Aquinas was regent master at the *studium provinciale* of [Santa Sabina](/wiki/Santa_Sabina "Santa Sabina") in Rome, the forerunner of the [Pontifical University of Saint Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Pontifical_University_of_Saint_Thomas_Aquinas "Pontifical University of Saint Thomas Aquinas"). Aquinas placed more emphasis on reason and argumentation, and was one of the first to use the new translation of Aristotle's metaphysical and epistemological writing. This was a significant departure from the [Neoplatonic](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism") and Augustinian thinking that had dominated much of early scholasticism. Aquinas showed how it was possible to incorporate much of the philosophy of Aristotle without falling into the "errors" of the Commentator, [Averroes](/wiki/Averroes "Averroes").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Philosopher Johann Beukes has suggested that from 1349 to 1464, the era between the deaths of [William of Ockham](/wiki/William_of_Ockham "William of Ockham") and [Nicholas of Cusa](/wiki/Nicholas_of_Cusa "Nicholas of Cusa"), there was a distinct period characterized by "robust and independent philosophers" who departed from high scholasticism on issues such as institutional criticism and materialism but retained scholasticism's method. These philosophers include [Marsilius of Padua](/wiki/Marsilius_of_Padua "Marsilius of Padua"), [Thomas Bradwardine](/wiki/Thomas_Bradwardine "Thomas Bradwardine"), [John Wycliffe](/wiki/John_Wycliffe "John Wycliffe"), [Catherine of Sienna](/wiki/Catherine_of_Sienna "Catherine of Sienna"), [Jean Gerson](/wiki/Jean_Gerson "Jean Gerson"), [Gabriel Biel](/wiki/Gabriel_Biel "Gabriel Biel") and ended with Nicholas of Cusa.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Thomistic scholasticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Thomistic scholasticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Antonio_granata%2C_san_tommaso_d%27aquino%2C_xviii_secolo.jpg/250px-Antonio_granata%2C_san_tommaso_d%27aquino%2C_xviii_secolo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antonio_granata,_san_tommaso_d%27aquino,_xviii_secolo.jpg)

Catholic philosopher [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas")

As J. A. Weisheipl [O.P.](/wiki/Order_of_Preachers "Order of Preachers") emphasizes, within the [Dominican Order](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") Thomistic scholasticism has been continuous since the time of Aquinas: "Thomism was always alive in the Dominican Order, small as it was after the ravages of the Reformation, the French Revolution, and the Napoleonic occupation. Repeated legislation of the General Chapters, beginning after the death of St. Thomas, as well as the Constitutions of the Order, required all Dominicans to teach the doctrine of St. Thomas both in philosophy and in theology."[[33]](#cite_note-33)

"Thomistic scholasticism" or "scholastic [Thomism](/wiki/Thomism "Thomism")" identifies with the philosophical and theological tradition stretching back to the time of St. Thomas Aquinas. It focuses not only on exegesis of the historical Aquinas but also on the articulation of a rigorous system of orthodox Thomism to be used as an instrument of critique of contemporary thought. Due to its suspicion of attempts to harmonize Aquinas with non-Thomistic categories and assumptions, scholastic Thomism has sometimes been called, according to philosophers like [Edward Feser](/wiki/Edward_Feser "Edward Feser"), "Strict Observance Thomism".[[34]](#cite_note-34) A discussion of recent and current Thomistic scholasticism can be found in *La Metafisica di san Tommaso d'Aquino e i suoi interpreti* (2002) by [Battista Mondin](/w/index.php?title=Battista_Mondin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Battista Mondin (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battista_Mondin "it:Battista Mondin")], which includes such figures as Sofia Vanni Rovighi (1908–1990),[[35]](#cite_note-35) [Cornelio Fabro](/wiki/Cornelio_Fabro "Cornelio Fabro") (1911–1995), Carlo Giacon (1900–1984),[[36]](#cite_note-36) [Tomas Tyn](/wiki/Tomas_Tyn "Tomas Tyn") [O.P.](/wiki/Order_of_Preachers "Order of Preachers") (1950–1990), Abelardo Lobato [O.P.](/wiki/Order_of_Preachers "Order of Preachers") (1925–2012), Leo Elders (1926– ) and [Giovanni Ventimiglia](/wiki/Ventimiglia_Giovanni "Ventimiglia Giovanni") (1964– ), among others. Fabro in particular emphasizes Aquinas' originality, especially with respect to the *actus essendi* or act of existence of finite beings by participating in being itself. Other scholars such as those involved with "Il Progetto Tommaso" seek to establish an objective and universal reading of Aquinas' texts.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Thomistic scholasticism in the English speaking world went into decline in the 1970s when the Thomistic revival that had been spearheaded by [Jacques Maritain](/wiki/Jacques_Maritain "Jacques Maritain"), [Étienne Gilson](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Gilson "Étienne Gilson"), and others, diminished in influence. Partly, this was because this branch of [Thomism](/wiki/Thomism "Thomism") had become a quest to understand the historical Aquinas after the [Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Second_Vatican_Council "Second Vatican Council").

### Analytical scholasticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Analytical scholasticism")]

A renewed interest in the "scholastic" way of doing philosophy has recently awoken within [analytic philosophy](/wiki/Analytic_philosophy "Analytic philosophy").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Attempts emerged to combine elements of scholastic and analytic methodology in pursuit of a contemporary philosophical synthesis. Proponents of various incarnations of this approach include [Anthony Kenny](/wiki/Anthony_Kenny "Anthony Kenny"), Peter King, Thomas Williams or [David Oderberg](/wiki/David_Oderberg "David Oderberg").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Scholastic method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Scholastic method")]

Cornelius O'Boyle explained that Scholasticism focuses on how to acquire knowledge and how to communicate effectively so that it may be acquired by others. It was thought that the best way to achieve this was by replicating the discovery process (*modus inveniendi*).[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The scholasticists would choose a book by a renowned scholar, *[auctor](/wiki/Author "Author")* (author), as a subject for investigation. By reading it thoroughly and critically, the disciples learned to appreciate the theories of the author. Other documents related to the book would be referenced, such as Church councils, papal letters and anything else written on the subject, be it ancient or contemporary. The points of disagreement and contention between multiple sources would be written down in individual sentences or snippets of text, known as [sententiae](/wiki/Sententiae "Sententiae"). Once the sources and points of disagreement had been laid out through a series of [dialectics](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic"), the two sides of an argument would be made whole so that they would be found to be in agreement and not contradictory. (Of course, sometimes opinions would be totally rejected, or new positions proposed.) This was done in two ways. The first was through [philological](/wiki/Philology "Philology") analysis. Words were examined and argued to have multiple meanings. It was also considered that the *auctor* might have intended a certain word to mean something different. Ambiguity could be used to find common ground between two otherwise contradictory statements. The second was through logical analysis, which relied on the rules of formal [logic](/wiki/Logic "Logic") – as they were known at the time – to show that contradictions did not exist but were subjective to the reader.[[41]](#cite_note-westintel-41)

## Scholastic instruction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scholasticism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Scholastic instruction")]

Scholastic instruction consisted of several elements. The first was the *lectio*: a teacher would read an authoritative text followed by a commentary, but no questions were permitted. This was followed by the *meditatio* ([meditation](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/meditatio "wikt:meditatio") or reflection) in which students reflected on and appropriated the text. Finally, in the *quaestio* students could ask questions (*quaestiones*) that might have occurred to them during *meditatio*. Eventually the discussion of *quaestiones* became a method of inquiry apart from the *lectio* and independent of authoritative texts. *Disputationes* were arranged to resolve controversial *quaestiones*.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Asselt201159-42)

Questions to be disputed were ordinarily announced beforehand, but students could propose a question to the teacher unannounced – *disputationes de quodlibet*. In this case, the teacher responded and the students rebutted; on the following day the teacher, having used notes taken during the disputation, summarised all arguments and presented his final position, riposting all rebuttals.[[41]](#cite_note-westintel-41)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Asselt201160-43)

The *quaestio* method of reasoning was initially used especially when two authoritative texts seemed to contradict one another. Two contradictory propositions would be considered in the form of an either/or question, and each part of the question would have to be approved (*sic*) or denied (*non*). Arguments for the position taken would be presented in turn, followed by arguments against the position, and finally the arguments against would be refuted. This method forced scholars to consider opposing viewpoints and defend their own arguments against them.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Asselt201161–62-44)
